.triad\13-codex-log.txt:55800:- Added SQLite-compatible ORM type variants for UUID/JSONB in `server/app/models.py` so DB-backed endpoint tests can run against in-memory SQLite without changing the Postgres migration.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:2:Ripgrep is not available. Falling back to GrepTool.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:9:- H2 (`showNotification` capability): I agree. Gating `showNotification` on `background-exec` is incorrect; it's a standard user-facing API that should not require special background permissions.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:11:- H4 (Plugin URL HTTPS not enforced): I agree. The plugin loader will fetch from any URL scheme, allowing for downgrade attacks and information leakage (which plugins are being used) on hostile networks.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:14:- M5 (`domStorageEnabled=false`): I agree with the assessment that this is an acceptable sandboxing choice but must be documented for plugin developers.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:16:- L3 (Email enumeration): I agree. The 409 response on signup leaks whether an email is registered.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:19:- M4 (`loadDataWithBaseURL` baseURL): Claude flags this for inspection but understates the risk. `PluginLoader.kt` uses `file:///plugin/index.html` as the base URL. Even with file access disabled, a `file://` origin has different security properties than an `https://` origin. This can be a vector for subtle sandbox escapes (e.g., via CORS policies or other origin-based checks). It should be changed to a standard `https://` dummy origin like `https://syncler-plugin-host`.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:23:  evidence: `PluginLoader.kt` uses `webView.addJavascriptInterface(bridge, ...)`. This is a known dangerous API. A malicious plugin can execute `window.__syncler_native__.getClass().forName("...")` to get a handle to arbitrary Java classes on the classpath and invoke their methods, completely bypassing the `PluginBridge`'s capability checks and escaping the sandbox.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:26:- **HIGH** **In-Memory Nonce Registry Allows Replay Attacks**:
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:27:  evidence: `server/app/crypto/nonce.py` and `docs/crypto-spec.md`. The `NonceRegistry` is an in-memory LRU cache. If the server restarts or runs multiple workers, there is no shared state. An attacker can trivially replay a message to a different worker or after a restart.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:28:  proposal: Implement the V1.5 plan mentioned in the spec immediately. Use a database or Redis-backed store for the nonce registry to ensure it is shared across all server processes and persists across restarts.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:31:  evidence: `server/app/middleware/rate_limit.py`. The `_increment_and_read_sqlite` function performs a non-atomic `SELECT` followed by an `UPDATE` or `INSERT`. This is a textbook Time-of-Check-to-Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) race condition that can allow an attacker to bypass rate limits under concurrent requests.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:34:- **HIGH** **Lack of Plugin Key Revocation Mechanism**:
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:35:  evidence: `PluginLoader.kt`. Plugin trust is anchored solely on the sender's public key. If a plugin developer's private signing key is compromised, an attacker can sign and distribute malicious updates to all users of that plugin, and there is no mechanism to revoke the compromised key.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:39:  evidence: `android/core/crypto/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/crypto/Aad.kt` and `server/app/crypto/aead.py`. The spec and Python implementation use sorted keys for canonical AAD JSON. The Kotlin implementation uses a hardcoded `linkedMapOf`. While the order currently matches alphabetical, this is fragile and will break if new fields are added out of order, causing all message decryption to fail.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:43:  evidence: `docs/crypto-spec.md` mentions the test vectors are asserted by `server/tests/test_crypto.py`. I can find no corresponding evidence of a test suite in the Android codebase that consumes and verifies these canonical vectors.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:52:- In-Memory Nonce Registry Allows Replay Attacks
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:55:- **Local Data Exposure**: The use of `android:allowBackup="false"` in the main `AndroidManifest.xml` and `EncryptedFile` in `AndroidEncryptedBundleStore` for plugin bundles at rest is excellent. Local data exposure seems well-mitigated.
.triad\25-gemini-review.txt:56:- **Plugin-to-Plugin Contamination**: The use of `android:process=":pluginhost"` combined with one `WebView` per plugin provides strong OS-level process isolation between plugins. The risk of one plugin's JS accessing another's is negligible.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:11:- `sdk-plugin/src/bridge.ts:57-60` catches all errors in `dispatchPluginHook` and **returns** `JSON.stringify(serializeError(error))` as a successful value.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:12:- `android/feature/plugin-host/.../PluginLoader.kt:271-281` (PluginHtmlShell.render) wraps the dispatcher and resolves on success or rejects on error.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:13:- Result: plugin errors silently arrive at the host as successful-looking results with a stringified error blob inside `.value`. Host can't distinguish "plugin ran fine and returned a string" from "plugin threw."
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:14:- **Fix:** make `dispatchPluginHook` re-throw on error so the M4b wrapper's `.catch` runs and the host sees `{error: "plugin_dispatch_failed", message: ...}`. Drop the try/catch in bridge.ts.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:16:**H2. `showNotification` gated by `background-exec` capability**
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:17:- `android/feature/plugin-host/.../PluginBridge.kt:84` maps `platform.showNotification` to `"background-exec"`.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:18:- Not in the Q21 capability list (network, storage, camera, gallery, file, location, background-exec). showNotification is part of the standard plugin surface — even render-only plugins should be able to post notifications when their plugin is invoked.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:19:- **Fix:** `requiredCapability` returns `null` for `platform.showNotification`. background-exec should gate whether the plugin's `onMessage` JS is allowed to run during push receipt — that's a different layer (M5's PluginNotificationService dispatch decision), not a per-call check.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:27:- `android/feature/plugin-host/.../PluginLoader.kt:75-85` (fetchBytesNoCache) accepts any URL scheme.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:28:- HTTP manifest URLs would happily be fetched. Signature still protects content, but network adversary can see which plugins are being installed.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:34:- `android/feature/plugin-host/.../PluginLoader.kt:243-244` (HTML shell `asResponse`) constructs `new Response(payload.body || "", ...)` where body is the bridge's serialized string.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:45:- **Fix:** wrap in a `@Singleton`-bound provider; declare in a `@Module` in `:feature:plugin-host`.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:48:- `PluginLoader.kt:201` uses `PluginWebViewClient.INITIAL_URL` as baseURL. If that's a `file://` URL, even though `allowFileAccess=false`, JS origin checks may treat it as file scheme. Need to confirm it's `https://syncler-plugin-host/` style.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:51:**M5. `domStorageEnabled = false, databaseEnabled = false` may break plugins using localStorage**
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:52:- `PluginLoader.kt:213-214` disables DOM storage and DB. M4a's `platform.storage` routes through the native bridge, but if a plugin transitively uses a library that touches localStorage, it'll fail.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:53:- **Fix:** acceptable — plugins must use `platform.storage`. Document in `docs/plugin-sandbox.md`.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:67:- Kotlin's `numberToJson` converts Doubles that equal an integer back to int string ("3" not "3.0"). Python's `json.dumps` writes "3.0" for floats. Manifests currently have no numeric fields, but future versions could.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:70:**L3. Email enumeration via signup 409**
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:71:- M1.4 returns 409 on duplicate email during signup. Combined with no email-existence check on login (M1.4 returns generic 401), this still leaks via signup: try signup with target email → 409 confirms existence.
.triad\25-claude-review-m1-m4b.md:77:- No top-level README explaining how the four directories (server, android, sdk-plugin, docs) relate.

.triad\16-codex-log.txt:54:- `assemble_aad(fields: dict) -> bytes` — canonical AAD assembly: sorted keys, JSON-canonical, UTF-8. .triad\16-codex-log.txt:80:4. **Message encryption**: AES-256-GCM with 12-byte random nonce per message. Wire format: nonce ‖ ciphertext_with_tag. AAD canonical JSON of `{message_id, sender_id, user_id, plugin_id, min_plugin_version, created_at, schema_version}`.
.triad\16-codex-log.txt:81:5. **Plugin bundle signing**: Ed25519. Sign `canonical_manifest_without_signature || bundleHash`. Canonical manifest = JSON with sorted keys, `separators=(",",":")`, ASCII only.
.triad\16-codex-log.txt:83:7. **Nonce reuse policy**: never reuse a nonce with the same key. Server-side LRU detects per-sender replay. Clients must use a CSPRNG.

.triad\31-gemini-review.txt:62:    -   **Verdict: Dangerous data loss.** The "remote-wins" strategy for `userScopedStorage` will discard local changes if the remote state has also changed. For example, if two offline devices both modify different keys within the same plugin's storage, the second device to sync will lose its changes entirely for that plugin's scope. This is a significant data integrity issue, even for a V1. The planned V1.5 fix (per-key timestamps) is necessary.
.triad\31-gemini-review.txt:65:    -   **Verdict: Safe.** The use of `optJSONArray` and `optJSONObject` combined with `.orEmpty()` default values in `EncryptedUserState.fromJson` ensures forward compatibility. An older state blob lacking newer fields will parse correctly into a model with empty lists/maps for the missing fields, preventing crashes and data corruption during schema evolution.
.triad\31-gemini-review.txt:68:    -   **Verdict: Correct.** The server-side code in `routers/state.py` consistently uses base64 encoding for `encrypted_blob` on both `GET` responses and in the `409 Conflict` body. The corresponding `decode_base64` on the `PUT` request ensures symmetry. The contract is clear and the test coverage reflects this.
.triad\31-gemini-review.txt:73:6.  **Server 409 returns the conflict body inline in `detail`. FastAPI's HTTPException stringifies dict detail. Is the client-side parsing path well-defined?**
.triad\31-gemini-review.txt:82:- 409 body parseability
.triad\29-codex-review.txt:8:4. Agree on signature binding. The public key is selected from `payload.sender_id`, so replaying another sender’s signature against this sender ID fails.
.triad\31-codex-review.txt:4:  [server/app/services/state.py](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:77) runs `UPDATE ... WHERE state_version = expected`, but never checks `rowcount` or uses `RETURNING`. If two clients read version `1`, both compute `new_version = 2`; one update wins, the other updates zero rows, commits, then [re-reads](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:88) version `2` and returns success. With SQLAlchemy’s identity map this re-read may be even less reliable. Fix by treating `rowcount == 0` / no `RETURNING` row as conflict, then rollback/expire and fetch current state for the 409 body.
.triad\31-codex-review.txt:7:  In the no-row path, both first writers with `expected_state_version = 0` can reach [db.add + commit](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:63). The primary key prevents double insert, so they will not both succeed, but the loser gets an unhandled `IntegrityError`/500 rather than a 409 with current state. Catch `IntegrityError`, rollback, read current, raise `StateConflictError`.
.triad\31-codex-review.txt:10:  [StateMerger.merge](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/StateMerger.kt:18) only has `local` and `remote`, no base/ancestor. For [userScopedStorage](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/StateMerger.kt:83), remote overwrites same keys and absent keys are treated as “unchanged”, not deletions. Since `StorageBridge` supports delete at [StorageBridge.kt](d:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/capabilities/StorageBridge.kt:44), a local delete will be resurrected if remote still has the key, and a local edit to the same key is dropped on conflict. Remote-wins is acceptable only if this storage is cache-like/disposable; for durable user plugin state, add per-key metadata/tombstones or exclude this class from V1 sync.
.triad\31-codex-review.txt:12:- **Medium: 409 DTO does not match the actual FastAPI error envelope.**  
.triad\31-codex-review.txt:13:  Server returns `{"detail": {"current_state_version": ..., "current_encrypted_blob": ...}}` from [routers/state.py](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/state.py:69). FastAPI does not stringify the dict; it nests it under `detail`. Android defines [StateConflictBodyDto](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/network/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/network/SynclerApi.kt:83) as if the body were top-level, so parsing `errorBody()` directly into that DTO will fail. Add a `StateConflictResponseDto(detail: StateConflictBodyDto)` or change the server to return a direct JSON response for 409.
.triad\31-codex-review.txt:16:  Top-level missing arrays/maps are safe via `optJSONArray`/`optJSONObject`, but missing `schema_version` defaults to `SCHEMA_V1` at [EncryptedUserState.kt](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/EncryptedUserState.kt:46), so older blobs are silently treated as v1 instead of routed through a v0 migration. .triad\28-codex-review-log.txt:3060:70:Missing AAD fields are errors. `min_plugin_version` is an empty string when unset (never null). `expires_at` is required and must be a future timestamp ≤ 30 days from the signing instant.
.triad\28-codex-review-log.txt:3066:88:> **V1 contract change (M5.1):** previous draft of this spec required `message_id`, `created_at`, and `schema_version` in AAD — those fields are server-generated and unknowable to the sender at signing time, so they have been removed from both AAD and the envelope. They remain server metadata stored next to the message but are not signed by the sender. AAD and the envelope are intentionally distinct: AAD authenticates context to AES-GCM (no ciphertext); the envelope is what Ed25519 signs (AAD + ciphertext + nonce). Replay protection is enforced at the server via the per-sender nonce registry (see §7).
.triad\28-codex-review-log.txt:3071:212:The V1 server performs a per-sender in-memory LRU replay check for the last 100,000 nonces. This protects the current server process only. A Redis or database-backed replay check is planned for V1.5.

.triad\32-codex-review.txt:3:- CRITICAL: `/v1/plugins/{id}/revoke` is unauthenticated. Anyone who knows a plugin UUID can revoke it; the route has only `db` dependency and no sender signature/auth check. See [plugins.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/plugins.py:120). This is a show-stopper for M8.
.triad\32-codex-review.txt:5:- HIGH: valid plugin upgrades cannot publish. `Plugin.id` is the table primary key [models.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/models.py:100), and `publish_plugin()` inserts each version with `id=_to_uuid(plugin_id)` [plugins.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/plugins.py:74). Publishing `1.0.1` after `1.0.0` for the same plugin passes the regression check, then hits the PK constraint and is mapped to 409. The tests only cover first publish and lower-version regression, not a successful higher-version update.
.triad\32-codex-review.txt:7:- HIGH: `IntegrityError -> VersionRegressionError` is too broad. Duplicate `(sender_id, version)` does map to 409 as intended [plugins.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/plugins.py:87), but the same handler also catches primary-key collisions, FK failures, and “same sender publishes different plugin at same version” under the M1.3 `UNIQUE(sender_id, version)` schema [models.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/models.py:98). The error text always says `(sender_id, version) already exists`, which will hide real schema/lifecycle bugs.
.triad\32-codex-review.txt:9:- MEDIUM: server/client version parsing is not exact for large numeric components. Server uses Python `int()` with unbounded size [plugins.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/plugins.py:36); Android uses `toInt()` [PluginVersionComparator.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/PluginVersionComparator.kt:40). A server-accepted version like a 12-digit major can later throw on Android.
.triad\32-codex-review.txt:11:- MEDIUM: `PendingUpdatesStore.deferUpdate()` is not monotonic and no consumer exists yet to suppress prompts. I found no UI/checker consumer of `remindAfterMs > now()`, and `PendingUpdatesStore` is also not bound in `StorageModule`, unlike `PairedSenderStore`.

.triad\31-codex-review-log.txt:3469:  [server/app/services/state.py](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:77) runs `UPDATE ... WHERE state_version = expected`, but never checks `rowcount` or uses `RETURNING`. If two clients read version `1`, both compute `new_version = 2`; one update wins, the other updates zero rows, commits, then [re-reads](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:88) version `2` and returns success. With SQLAlchemy’s identity map this re-read may be even less reliable. Fix by treating `rowcount == 0` / no `RETURNING` row as conflict, then rollback/expire and fetch current state for the 409 body.
.triad\31-codex-review-log.txt:3472:  In the no-row path, both first writers with `expected_state_version = 0` can reach [db.add + commit](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:63). The primary key prevents double insert, so they will not both succeed, but the loser gets an unhandled `IntegrityError`/500 rather than a 409 with current state. Catch `IntegrityError`, rollback, read current, raise `StateConflictError`.
.triad\31-codex-review-log.txt:3475:  [StateMerger.merge](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/StateMerger.kt:18) only has `local` and `remote`, no base/ancestor. For [userScopedStorage](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/StateMerger.kt:83), remote overwrites same keys and absent keys are treated as “unchanged”, not deletions. Since `StorageBridge` supports delete at [StorageBridge.kt](d:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/capabilities/StorageBridge.kt:44), a local delete will be resurrected if remote still has the key, and a local edit to the same key is dropped on conflict. Remote-wins is acceptable only if this storage is cache-like/disposable; for durable user plugin state, add per-key metadata/tombstones or exclude this class from V1 sync.
.triad\31-codex-review-log.txt:3477:- **Medium: 409 DTO does not match the actual FastAPI error envelope.**  
.triad\31-codex-review-log.txt:3478:  Server returns `{"detail": {"current_state_version": ..., "current_encrypted_blob": ...}}` from [routers/state.py](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/state.py:69). FastAPI does not stringify the dict; it nests it under `detail`. Android defines [StateConflictBodyDto](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/network/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/network/SynclerApi.kt:83) as if the body were top-level, so parsing `errorBody()` directly into that DTO will fail. Add a `StateConflictResponseDto(detail: StateConflictBodyDto)` or change the server to return a direct JSON response for 409.
.triad\31-codex-review-log.txt:3481:  Top-level missing arrays/maps are safe via `optJSONArray`/`optJSONObject`, but missing `schema_version` defaults to `SCHEMA_V1` at [EncryptedUserState.kt](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/EncryptedUserState.kt:46), so older blobs are silently treated as v1 instead of routed through a v0 migration. Nested records still use required `getString` calls, for example [installed plugin fields](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/EncryptedUserState.kt:83) and [modified_at](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/EncryptedUserState.kt:126), so older partial entries can still throw.
.triad\31-codex-review-log.txt:3490:6. **409 parseability:** server body is valid JSON but nested under `detail`; Android needs an envelope DTO or server should return a direct body.

.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:27:- `app/services/messages.py` — store_message (validates pairing, plugin, devices; 410 if none), inbox_for_device, get_message_for_user, mark_dismissed; encrypted_body_pointer "inline:" scheme
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:28:- `app/services/push.py` — Firebase Admin lazy init; dev-mode no-op; FCM data payload contains ONLY (type, message_id, plugin_id, min_plugin_version) — never plaintext
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:46:1. **Envelope canonical bytes.** `routers/messages.py` builds `envelope_bytes` from `{sender_id, user_id, plugin_id, encrypted_body, nonce, min_plugin_version}` with sort_keys + separators(",", ":"). Sender SDK (not yet written — M9) MUST produce byte-identical bytes when signing. Is the field set complete? Should `expires_at` or a server-generated `message_id` be in the AAD/envelope? Trade-off: more fields = more replay protection, fewer fields = sender doesn't need to predict server state.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:48:2. **AAD vs envelope.** The crypto-spec defines AAD = (message_id, sender_id, user_id, plugin_id, min_plugin_version, created_at, schema_version). But message_id is server-generated AFTER the sender signs. How does the sender include message_id in AAD if they don't know it yet? Either the AAD must be relaxed for V1 OR the sender generates a client-side message_id and the server adopts it. Currently the schema_version + created_at + message_id fields are likely DROPPED in my impl. Check.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:50:3. **Replay window on /send.** Server doesn't dedupe by anything sender-supplied (e.g., idempotency_key). A network adversary intercepting one message can replay it. Server's `NonceRegistry` (M2) is in-memory only; tests use a fresh process. Real impact = duplicate delivery (plugin handles). Confirmed acceptable per Round 11 fix-up notes?
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:56:6. **Foreground service uptime budget.** PluginNotificationService has MAX_WORK_MS = 30s. If plugin code hangs, service aborts and user gets nothing. Is 30s the right number? Should this surface a "delivery failed" notification on timeout?

.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:4526:.triad\01-brief.md:19:Emotional stake: this is clearly an idea the user is excited about. The conversation arc shows them getting more ambitious as we talked, which is both the energy that will sustain the project and the risk vector that may make them over-scope it. They explicitly said V1 should "ship perfect, ready for everything, kill it." They believe AI tooling (specifically Claude) compresses an 18-month build into 2 weeks. I pushed back on both of these; my pushback is in the record above but should not be assumed to have changed their mind.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:4527:.triad\01-brief.md:28:- Expanded to: "the phone app should be programmable too — senders register renderer classes that know how to display their custom payloads." This is a plugin architecture. I flagged the standard plugin-API design risk (you can't change the contract after the fact) and argued for declarative-first, plugins-later. User pushed back, citing the .exe trust model; I conceded the trust model is fine if installation is user-initiated, and stuck to the V1/V2 argument as a design concern, not a safety concern.

.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:4752:.triad\05-triad-vote.md:21:**Reason:** Sender publishes a QR encoding `{ plugin_manifest_url, one_time_pairing_token, sender_public_key_fingerprint }`. User scans with their phone app → app fetches the manifest → verifies signature → shows install confirmation with declared capabilities → user taps install → app generates user keypair, exchanges with sender's server via the one-time token → pairing done. Why QR over URL-paste: (a) phone is already in hand, (b) one-time token can be short-lived and embedded, (c) prevents typo-domain phishing. URL-paste (option b from user's choices) is a fine fallback for headless contexts but QR is the V1 default.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:4756:.triad\05-triad-vote.md:27:**Reason:** In V1, plugins are installed by URL — no review gate catches a malicious update. Auto-update would let a compromised sender push code to user's phone silently. Prompt-to-update is one tap; user is the only user; not annoying. When marketplace ships V1.5, re-review on each version becomes the trust anchor, and auto-update is safe again.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:4761:.triad\06-codex-log.txt:127:The zero-knowledge multi-device model is sound, but the key hierarchy needs to be explicit: password-derived key unlocks an encrypted user master key; sender pairings and message keys hang off that, not directly off the password. Broker pairing fits multi-device if pairing attaches to the user account/master public identity, then syncs encrypted sender state to every device. `platform.showNotification(...)` creates duplicate notification and action risks across devices; V1 needs account-level message IDs and sender-side idempotency so two phones cannot accidentally submit the same action twice. Plugin version pinning still works, but every device must be able to fetch/cache the exact plugin version required to render or handle a message.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:4762:.triad\06-codex-out.txt:2:The zero-knowledge multi-device model is sound, but the key hierarchy needs to be explicit: password-derived key unlocks an encrypted user master key; sender pairings and message keys hang off that, not directly off the password. Plugin version pinning still works, but every device must be able to fetch/cache the exact plugin version required to render or handle a message.

.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:7023:.triad\27-review-m5-prompt.md:33:1. **Envelope canonical bytes.** `routers/messages.py` builds `envelope_bytes` from `{sender_id, user_id, plugin_id, encrypted_body, nonce, min_plugin_version}` with sort_keys + separators(",", ":"). Sender SDK (not yet written — M9) MUST produce byte-identical bytes when signing. Is the field set complete? Should `expires_at` or a server-generated `message_id` be in the AAD/envelope? Trade-off: more fields = more replay protection, fewer fields = sender doesn't need to predict server state.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:7024:.triad\27-review-m5-prompt.md:35:2. **AAD vs envelope.** The crypto-spec defines AAD = (message_id, sender_id, user_id, plugin_id, min_plugin_version, created_at, schema_version). But message_id is server-generated AFTER the sender signs. How does the sender include message_id in AAD if they don't know it yet? Either the AAD must be relaxed for V1 OR the sender generates a client-side message_id and the server adopts it. Currently the schema_version + created_at + message_id fields are likely DROPPED in my impl. Check.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:7027:.triad\27-gemini-review.txt:7:- **1. Envelope canonical bytes:** Concur. `expires_at` is part of the request but not the signed envelope, allowing an attacker to replay a message long after its intended expiry. It should be added to the envelope.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:7028:.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:46:1. **Envelope canonical bytes.** `routers/messages.py` builds `envelope_bytes` from `{sender_id, user_id, plugin_id, encrypted_body, nonce, min_plugin_version}` with sort_keys + separators(",", ":"). Sender SDK (not yet written — M9) MUST produce byte-identical bytes when signing. Is the field set complete? Should `expires_at` or a server-generated `message_id` be in the AAD/envelope? Trade-off: more fields = more replay protection, fewer fields = sender doesn't need to predict server state.
.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:7029:.triad\27-codex-review-log.txt:48:2. **AAD vs envelope.** The crypto-spec defines AAD = (message_id, sender_id, user_id, plugin_id, min_plugin_version, created_at, schema_version). But message_id is server-generated AFTER the sender signs. How does the sender include message_id in AAD if they don't know it yet? Either the AAD must be relaxed for V1 OR the sender generates a client-side message_id and the server adopts it. Currently the schema_version + created_at + message_id fields are likely DROPPED in my impl. Check.

.triad\33-codex-review.txt:4:[docs/lottery-integration-spec.md](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/lottery-integration-spec.md:92) tells the plugin to call `platform.message.respond('played', ...)`, then expects `onAction` to POST to the lottery backend at [line 100](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/lottery-integration-spec.md:100). But the Android bridge currently just validates `actionId` and returns `{}` in [MessageBridge.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/capabilities/MessageBridge.kt:11); it does not dispatch `onAction`, forward the payload, or inject `device_id`. A Claude instance following this doc will build an integration where tapping “Played” never reaches `/api/played`.
.triad\33-codex-review.txt:6:[P1] The lottery plugin signing instructions produce a signature Android will reject.  
.triad\33-codex-review.txt:7:The doc points to `node .../tools/sign-bundle.js --bundle ...` at [docs/lottery-integration-spec.md](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/lottery-integration-spec.md:126), but the actual tool expects positional args and is TypeScript, not that JS/flag shape: [sign-bundle.ts](D:/Projects/syncler/sdk-plugin/tools/sign-bundle.ts:14). More importantly, the tool signs the raw bundle at [line 23](D:/Projects/syncler/sdk-plugin/tools/sign-bundle.ts:23), while Android verifies `canonicalManifestWithoutSignature || bundleHash` at [PluginSignatureVerifier.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/PluginSignatureVerifier.kt:31). The doc then publishes that signature at [docs/lottery-integration-spec.md](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/lottery-integration-spec.md:159), so the published plugin can’t pass device-side signature verification.
.triad\33-codex-review.txt:9:[P2] SDK/server signing bytes match only if UUID strings are already server-canonical.  
.triad\33-codex-review.txt:10:`send_to` signs `self.sender_id`, `user_uuid`, and `plugin_id` exactly as caller strings at [client.py](D:/Projects/syncler/sdk-python/syncler/client.py:229), while the server rebuilds the envelope from Pydantic UUIDs using `str(payload.sender_id/user_id/plugin_id)` at [messages.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/messages.py:67). Same issue exists for plugin publish: SDK signs `self.sender_id` at [client.py](D:/Projects/syncler/sdk-python/syncler/client.py:283), server signs against `str(payload.sender_id)` at [plugins.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/plugins.py:48). Uppercase/braced/URN UUIDs are valid inputs server-side but normalize before verification, causing `401 invalid signature`. Canonicalize UUID inputs in the SDK before signing and sending.

This correctly handles the race condition where two identical versions are published concurrently.

.triad\32-codex-review-log.txt:3753:4. Agree on signature binding. The public key is selected from `payload.sender_id`, so replaying another sender‚?Ts signature against this sender ID fails.
.triad\32-codex-review-log.txt:3821:  [server/app/services/state.py](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:77) runs `UPDATE ... WHERE state_version = expected`, but never checks `rowcount` or uses `RETURNING`. If two clients read version `1`, both compute `new_version = 2`; one update wins, the other updates zero rows, commits, then [re-reads](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:88) version `2` and returns success. With SQLAlchemy‚?Ts identity map this re-read may be even less reliable. Fix by treating `rowcount == 0` / no `RETURNING` row as conflict, then rollback/expire and fetch current state for the 409 body.
.triad\32-codex-review-log.txt:3824:  In the no-row path, both first writers with `expected_state_version = 0` can reach [db.add + commit](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:63). The primary key prevents double insert, so they will not both succeed, but the loser gets an unhandled `IntegrityError`/500 rather than a 409 with current state. Catch `IntegrityError`, rollback, read current, raise `StateConflictError`.
.triad\32-codex-review-log.txt:3827:  [StateMerger.merge](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/StateMerger.kt:18) only has `local` and `remote`, no base/ancestor. For [userScopedStorage](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/StateMerger.kt:83), remote overwrites same keys and absent keys are treated as ‚??unchanged‚??, not deletions. Since `StorageBridge` supports delete at [StorageBridge.kt](d:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/capabilities/StorageBridge.kt:44), a local delete will be resurrected if remote still has the key, and a local edit to the same key is dropped on conflict. Remote-wins is acceptable only if this storage is cache-like/disposable; for durable user plugin state, add per-key metadata/tombstones or exclude this class from V1 sync.
.triad\32-codex-review-log.txt:3829:- **Medium: 409 DTO does not match the actual FastAPI error envelope.**  
.triad\32-codex-review-log.txt:3830:  Server returns `{"detail": {"current_state_version": ..., "current_encrypted_blob": ...}}` from [routers/state.py](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/state.py:69). FastAPI does not stringify the dict; it nests it under `detail`. Android defines [StateConflictBodyDto](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/network/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/network/SynclerApi.kt:83) as if the body were top-level, so parsing `errorBody()` directly into that DTO will fail. Add a `StateConflictResponseDto(detail: StateConflictBodyDto)` or change the server to return a direct JSON response for 409.
.triad\32-codex-review-log.txt:3833:  Top-level missing arrays/maps are safe via `optJSONArray`/`optJSONObject`, but missing `schema_version` defaults to `SCHEMA_V1` at [EncryptedUserState.kt](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/EncryptedUserState.kt:46), so older blobs are silently treated as v1 instead of routed through a v0 migration. Nested records still use required `getString` calls, for example [installed plugin fields](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/EncryptedUserState.kt:83) and [modified_at](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/EncryptedUserState.kt:126), so older partial entries can still throw.
.triad\32-codex-review-log.txt:3842:6. **409 parseability:** server body is valid JSON but nested under `detail`; Android needs an envelope DTO or server should return a direct body.

.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:16:End-to-end dogfood works: paired sender publishes a plugin bundle, sender pushes a card, phone polls, decrypts, fetches+verifies the bundle by SHA-256, renders the plugin's HTML in a per-card WebView, action buttons hit the sender's callback through a hardened native bridge. The Android UX is currently a placeholder built only to prove the pipeline:
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:21:- No concept of read/unread, no grouping, no search, no detail view, no per-plugin organization
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:22:- Cards above the fold get clipped; rich plugins (20 lines of detail + buttons + summary) are unusable
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:30:1. **Card presentation.** Fixed-height vs auto-grow vs max-height+scroll vs preview+detail. Who owns height — the host or the plugin (via manifest hint, via measurement-on-render, via a separate `previewHeight` field, via the plugin telling the host "I'm done rendering and I'm N pixels tall")?
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:34:3. **Organization / hierarchy.** Flat list, grouped by plugin (collapsible sections), bottom-nav tabs per plugin, date buckets (Today / Yesterday / Earlier), pinned/starred, plugin-owned mini-tabs. Trade-offs?
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:36:4. **Search.** The user's intuition was "maybe search is implemented BY the plugin — the host shows a search UI only if the plugin opts in, the host forwards the query to the plugin, the plugin queries against its backend, plugin renders results." Pressure-test this. Alternatives: host-side metadata search (sender, plugin id, sent-at) vs plugin-side full search vs hybrid. What's the right boundary?
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:38:5. **Plugin role expansion.** V1 plugins own the card render only. Future possibilities: plugins own the detail view, plugins own a dedicated section/tab, plugins can register additional surfaces (settings, history view, dashboards). What's the smallest expansion that makes the inbox usable for rich plugins, and where do we draw the line so plugins don't take over the chrome?
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:40:6. **Lifecycle.** Server retention cap is 30 days. Client can persist longer (the encrypted body + plugin bundle are reproducible from the inbox + plugin manifest). Dismissal protocol exists (DISMISS_ALL etc.) but isn't wired to UI. Archive vs delete? Auto-expire?
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:42:7. **App chrome.** Bottom-nav, top-app-bar, FAB? Should the chrome itself be plugin-aware (e.g., a "compose" affordance only makes sense for plugins that opt in)?
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:44:8. **Edge cases that need first-class treatment.** Empty inbox, fresh-pair "what do I do now", plugin fetch failed, plugin render threw, plugin bundle revoked since the message was stored, payload doesn't validate against current plugin version.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:53:- **Where the lottery sender's needs differ from a future trading-bot / notification / chat-style sender** (the plugin space is intentionally open — your UX has to serve all of them, not just lottery)
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:54:- **The plugin contract changes** the SDK would need to support your direction (new manifest fields? new dispatch hooks? new platform APIs?) — be specific
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:57:Tone: opinionated. The user wants discussion, not a shopping list. If you think the host should NOT own grouping and search at all, say that and explain. If you think the plugin should be allowed to take over half the chrome, defend it.

.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:222:- **P0: `NetworkBridge` release HTTP relaxation is bypassed in the existing plugin host.**  
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:223:  [NetworkBridge.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/capabilities/NetworkBridge.kt:21) only applies `MODERN_TLS` in release when it constructs its own client. But [PluginLoader.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/PluginLoader.kt:107) injects a default OkHttp client, so release `platform.network.fetch` can still use OkHttpג€™s default cleartext-capable specs if the manifest declares an `http://` endpoint. Fix by enforcing scheme in `fetch()` itself, or always wrapping injected clients with release-safe `connectionSpecs`.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:228:- **P1: AAD reconstruction is byte-correct for the Python SDK, but timestamp canonicalization is fragile.**  
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:229:  Android uses `dto.expiresAt` directly in AAD at [InboxRepository.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/inbox/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/feature/inbox/InboxRepository.kt:110). The server verifies using `payload.expires_at.isoformat().replace("+00:00", "Z")` at [messages.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/messages.py:65), then later returns the DB datetime at [messages.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/messages.py:246). If any sender uses a non-UTC offset string that the server accepts, Postgres may normalize it before inbox response and device decrypt will fail. For tonightג€™s Python SDK dogfood, `Z` output is okay. For protocol safety, reject non-UTC `expires_at` or persist/return the exact canonical AAD string.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:231:- **P1: Docs still contradict V1 action callback behavior.**  
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:232:  [integration-guide.md](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/integration-guide.md:42) says `device_id` is in the callback body and [line 46](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/integration-guide.md:46) says dedupe by `(item_id, device_id)`, but [line 218](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/integration-guide.md:218) correctly says V1 does not inject `device_id`. The Flask example still reads `data["device_id"]` at [line 229](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/integration-guide.md:229). This will break a sender following the guide.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:235:  `sdk-plugin/examples/minimal/build.sh` is fixed, but `npm run build:example` in [package.json](D:/Projects/syncler/sdk-plugin/package.json:15) still uses `--format=esm`. Anyone using that script gets the old non-module `<script>` syntax error.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:239:- `plugin_publish` rate limiting is shaped correctly: the per-sender bucket fires only after sender lookup and signature verification, so spoofers cannot burn another senderג€™s bucket.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:241:- `plugin_identifier` projection is batch-loaded for inbox and falls back safely.

.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:288:  [server/app/services/state.py](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:77) runs `UPDATE ... WHERE state_version = expected`, but never checks `rowcount` or uses `RETURNING`. If two clients read version `1`, both compute `new_version = 2`; one update wins, the other updates zero rows, commits, then [re-reads](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:88) version `2` and returns success. With SQLAlchemyג€™s identity map this re-read may be even less reliable. Fix by treating `rowcount == 0` / no `RETURNING` row as conflict, then rollback/expire and fetch current state for the 409 body.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:291:  In the no-row path, both first writers with `expected_state_version = 0` can reach [db.add + commit](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/services/state.py:63). The primary key prevents double insert, so they will not both succeed, but the loser gets an unhandled `IntegrityError`/500 rather than a 409 with current state. Catch `IntegrityError`, rollback, read current, raise `StateConflictError`.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:294:  [StateMerger.merge](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/StateMerger.kt:18) only has `local` and `remote`, no base/ancestor. Since `StorageBridge` supports delete at [StorageBridge.kt](d:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/capabilities/StorageBridge.kt:44), a local delete will be resurrected if remote still has the key, and a local edit to the same key is dropped on conflict. .triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:296:- **Medium: 409 DTO does not match the actual FastAPI error envelope.**  
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:297:  Server returns `{"detail": {"current_state_version": ..., "current_encrypted_blob": ...}}` from [routers/state.py](d:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/state.py:69). FastAPI does not stringify the dict; it nests it under `detail`. Android defines [StateConflictBodyDto](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/network/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/network/SynclerApi.kt:83) as if the body were top-level, so parsing `errorBody()` directly into that DTO will fail. .triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:300:  Top-level missing arrays/maps are safe via `optJSONArray`/`optJSONObject`, but missing `schema_version` defaults to `SCHEMA_V1` at [EncryptedUserState.kt](d:/Projects/syncler/android/core/storage/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/core/storage/EncryptedUserState.kt:46), so older blobs are silently treated as v1 instead of routed through a v0 migration. .triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:309:6. **409 parseability:** server body is valid JSON but nested under `detail`; Android needs an envelope DTO or server should return a direct body.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:318:**host-owned inbox item, plugin-owned detail surface.**
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:320:The host should own the list, unread state, grouping, archive, expiration, selection, empty/error states, and app chrome. Plugins should own the rich rendering and domain-specific interaction once the user opens a message or enters a plugin-owned surface. That gives you a usable inbox without letting arbitrary plugins fragment the core app into multiple competing UX systems.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:322:The current fixed `280 dp` WebView is not merely the wrong height. It is the wrong contract. A rich card with twenty lines of detail, summary, and buttons does not belong inline in a feed. Auto-growing it would make the feed unusable. Nested scroll would make gesture behavior poor. Letting the plugin dynamically claim height would turn the host list into a layout negotiation protocol with untrusted, asynchronous documents. That path gets fragile quickly.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:327:2. **Message detail view**, rendered by the plugin in a WebView.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:329:The preview should be compact, predictable, and scannable: sender/plugin identity, time, title, short summary, unread marker, status/error affordance, maybe one primary action if the plugin declares it safe. The detail view is where the current plugin HTML belongs.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:340:The plugin should not be able to say “I am 842 px tall, resize me.” That is fine in a detail surface, not in the inbox. Measurement-on-render and “done rendering” callbacks are especially dangerous because they couple list virtualization, WebView lifecycle, and untrusted content timing. The UX will jitter, scroll positions will jump, and failures become layout bugs.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:342:The plugin contract should instead separate **preview model** from **detail renderer**.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:344:A message should carry encrypted, client-visible host metadata, produced by the sender/plugin SDK:
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:358:The host renders that natively. Then the detail WebView gets the full payload and plugin bundle. This is the clean boundary: the server remains content-blind, but the client can use decrypted metadata.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:360:I would still allow optional plugin-rendered preview later, but not as the default. If you allow it, constrain it brutally: fixed host-provided viewport, no dynamic height, no network during preview render unless explicitly allowed, no arbitrary chrome, no assumption that it appears for every row. The first usable version should not depend on WebView-per-row at all.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:386:- or the plugin requests `markRead()` during detail view after a host-approved event.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:388:That last one matters for chat-style or ticket-style plugins, where the detail view may have its own “seen” semantics. But the host should still mediate it.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:390:Visual treatment should be boring and strong: unread dot, heavier title, maybe a small unread count in date/plugin filters. Avoid giant blue backgrounds or card chrome that makes the whole inbox noisy. “Unread” is a state, not the main content.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:403:Then add lightweight host filters: all, unread, starred/pinned, archived, maybe plugin/sender chips. I would not start with bottom-nav tabs per plugin. That makes the app feel like a plugin launcher instead of an inbox, and it breaks down when one plugin is chat-like, another is alert-like, and another is a dashboard.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:405:Grouped-by-plugin collapsible sections sound appealing for lottery because the domain is discrete. They are worse for notification streams, trading bots, and chat-like senders where recency matters more than plugin identity. Use plugin grouping as a filter or secondary view, not the primary hierarchy.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:407:Pinned/starred is worth having, but not before archive/read/detail. Date buckets are cheap and broadly useful. Plugin-owned mini-tabs inside detail are acceptable because once the user is inside a message/plugin surface, domain organization is the plugin’s job.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:413:- **Devices/Settings**: pairing, senders, plugin permissions, account/session.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:415:Do not add a plugin tab bar yet. If plugins later register durable sections, they can appear behind a “Plugins” or “Sources” area, not as peers to Inbox on day one.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:419:The user’s instinct that “search may be implemented by the plugin” is half right, but dangerous as the only model.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:423:1. **Host metadata search**: sender, plugin id/name, title, subtitle, summary, sent-at, unread/archive/starred.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:424:2. **Local decrypted message search**: host-indexed `searchText` supplied by the plugin/sender.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:425:3. **Plugin remote search**: plugin queries its backend and renders results.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:429:The host should provide a global search box that always works over local metadata. Because the server is content-blind, the index is local and built after decrypt. The plugin should be able to supply search tokens or a plain text search blob in the encrypted payload. That gives users a reliable search for “Powerball,” “ticket,” “AAPL,” “withdrawal,” or “Alice” without waking every plugin.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:431:Plugin-side remote search should be an optional extension, not the core inbox search. It is useful for domains where the inbox is only a notification layer over a larger backend: trading history, old chat messages, ticket databases, dashboards. But if search only works when the plugin opts in and its backend is available, the host inbox feels broken. Also, plugin-rendered search results raise navigation questions: are they inbox messages, remote entities, or plugin pages?
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:437:- Results are visually separated as “From this device” and “From plugin/service.”
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:438:- Remote plugin results open plugin-owned detail/history surfaces, not fake inbox messages unless the sender actually emitted a message.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:440:That preserves the inbox as a reliable local object while letting richer plugins grow.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:444:The smallest expansion that makes rich plugins usable is not “plugins own tabs.” It is:
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:452:Next, I would allow a plugin-owned **history/detail section** only after the host has stable Inbox and Archive concepts. A plugin could register:
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:460:But the host must keep the app chrome. Plugins can own content panes, not global navigation rules. They should not be able to replace the top app bar, hide archive/read controls, spoof pairing UI, or create their own permission prompts. The plugin can ask the host to show chrome actions: `markUnread`, `archive`, `openSettings`, `compose`, `refresh`, but the host renders them.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:462:The line I would draw: plugins can own surfaces; the host owns containment, navigation, identity, and trust signals.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:466:Retention changes the UX. A 30-day server cap means the app needs an explicit distinction between server-backed inbox and local history.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:479:Client persistence longer than 30 days is valuable, especially for lottery and trading. But the plugin bundle question matters: if rendering old messages depends on a bundle that was revoked or unavailable, history breaks. Store the verified bundle version used for the message, or store a stable render artifact/manifest reference. At minimum, cache bundles by hash, not just plugin latest.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:487:- Optional FAB only if there is at least one installed/paired plugin with a host-declared compose capability.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:489:The chrome can be plugin-aware, but only through host-declared capabilities. For example, a trading bot might expose “New alert,” a chat plugin might expose “New message,” lottery might expose nothing. If multiple plugins expose compose, the FAB opens a chooser. If none do, no FAB.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:499:Plugin fetch failed should still show a host preview if metadata decrypted. The row opens an error detail with retry, plugin id/version/hash, and safe diagnostic text. The user should be able to archive/delete without the plugin rendering.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:505:Payload does not validate against current plugin version: do not use current plugin version. Use the message’s declared plugin id, version/range, bundle hash, and schema. If the exact compatible bundle is unavailable, show a compatibility error and preserve the message.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:511:A trading bot is more time-sensitive and risk-sensitive. It needs priority, freshness, maybe pinned alerts, quick actions, audit history, and stronger stale-state warnings. A trading alert from 27 minutes ago may be materially different from a lottery result from yesterday. It may also need plugin remote search/history because the backend has orders, fills, and account state beyond messages.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:515:A chat-style sender breaks the “card” metaphor. It wants conversation grouping, sender identity, read receipts, compose, and thread-level navigation. That is exactly why the host should not hard-code plugin grouping as the main inbox model. The host can support a message detail or plugin section that becomes a conversation view, but the global inbox should remain a sane notification surface.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:519:I would add these plugin/message contract pieces:
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:523:- `surfaces` in plugin manifest: `messageDetail`, optional `previewRenderer`, optional `settings`, optional `history`, optional `compose`, optional `searchProvider`.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:527:- Search API: `plugin.search(query, cursor)` returning typed results, with host-rendered result shells and plugin-owned result detail.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:528:- Error contract: plugin can emit validation failure with user-safe message and diagnostic code.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:536:The native bridge currently only has `platform.network.fetch` in inbox mode. Detail surfaces need lifecycle and host state APIs, but those APIs must be narrow and auditable. Do not expand by handing plugins general navigation control.
.triad\35-codex-ux-log.txt:540:The product should become an inbox first and a plugin host second. That does not make plugins weak. It gives them the right place to be powerful: inside detail, history, compose, and dashboard surfaces, contained by host chrome that users can trust.
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:6:- **P0: `NetworkBridge` release HTTP relaxation is bypassed in the existing plugin host.**  
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:7:  [NetworkBridge.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/capabilities/NetworkBridge.kt:21) only applies `MODERN_TLS` in release when it constructs its own client. But [PluginLoader.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/plugin-host/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/android/pluginhost/PluginLoader.kt:107) injects a default OkHttp client, so release `platform.network.fetch` can still use OkHttp’s default cleartext-capable specs if the manifest declares an `http://` endpoint. Fix by enforcing scheme in `fetch()` itself, or always wrapping injected clients with release-safe `connectionSpecs`.
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:12:- **P1: AAD reconstruction is byte-correct for the Python SDK, but timestamp canonicalization is fragile.**  
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:13:  Android uses `dto.expiresAt` directly in AAD at [InboxRepository.kt](D:/Projects/syncler/android/feature/inbox/src/main/kotlin/app/syncler/feature/inbox/InboxRepository.kt:110). The server verifies using `payload.expires_at.isoformat().replace("+00:00", "Z")` at [messages.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/messages.py:65), then later returns the DB datetime at [messages.py](D:/Projects/syncler/server/app/routers/messages.py:246). If any sender uses a non-UTC offset string that the server accepts, Postgres may normalize it before inbox response and device decrypt will fail. For tonight’s Python SDK dogfood, `Z` output is okay. For protocol safety, reject non-UTC `expires_at` or persist/return the exact canonical AAD string.
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:15:- **P1: Docs still contradict V1 action callback behavior.**  
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:16:  [integration-guide.md](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/integration-guide.md:42) says `device_id` is in the callback body and [line 46](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/integration-guide.md:46) says dedupe by `(item_id, device_id)`, but [line 218](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/integration-guide.md:218) correctly says V1 does not inject `device_id`. The Flask example still reads `data["device_id"]` at [line 229](D:/Projects/syncler/docs/integration-guide.md:229). This will break a sender following the guide.
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:19:  `sdk-plugin/examples/minimal/build.sh` is fixed, but `npm run build:example` in [package.json](D:/Projects/syncler/sdk-plugin/package.json:15) still uses `--format=esm`. Anyone using that script gets the old non-module `<script>` syntax error.
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:23:- `plugin_publish` rate limiting is shaped correctly: the per-sender bucket fires only after sender lookup and signature verification, so spoofers cannot burn another sender’s bucket.
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:25:- `plugin_identifier` projection is batch-loaded for inbox and falls back safely.
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:26:- `min_plugin_version` null-to-empty handling matches the Python SDK AAD shape.
.triad\34-codex-review.txt:27:- `npm run build` in `sdk-plugin` passes.

.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:5:**host-owned inbox item, plugin-owned detail surface.**
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:7:The host should own the list, unread state, grouping, archive, expiration, selection, empty/error states, and app chrome. Plugins should own the rich rendering and domain-specific interaction once the user opens a message or enters a plugin-owned surface. That gives you a usable inbox without letting arbitrary plugins fragment the core app into multiple competing UX systems.
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:9:The current fixed `280 dp` WebView is not merely the wrong height. It is the wrong contract. A rich card with twenty lines of detail, summary, and buttons does not belong inline in a feed. Auto-growing it would make the feed unusable. Nested scroll would make gesture behavior poor. Letting the plugin dynamically claim height would turn the host list into a layout negotiation protocol with untrusted, asynchronous documents. That path gets fragile quickly.
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:14:2. **Message detail view**, rendered by the plugin in a WebView.
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:16:The preview should be compact, predictable, and scannable: sender/plugin identity, time, title, short summary, unread marker, status/error affordance, maybe one primary action if the plugin declares it safe. The detail view is where the current plugin HTML belongs.
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:27:The plugin should not be able to say “I am 842 px tall, resize me.” That is fine in a detail surface, not in the inbox. Measurement-on-render and “done rendering” callbacks are especially dangerous because they couple list virtualization, WebView lifecycle, and untrusted content timing. The UX will jitter, scroll positions will jump, and failures become layout bugs.
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:29:The plugin contract should instead separate **preview model** from **detail renderer**.
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:31:A message should carry encrypted, client-visible host metadata, produced by the sender/plugin SDK:
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:45:The host renders that natively. Then the detail WebView gets the full payload and plugin bundle. This is the clean boundary: the server remains content-blind, but the client can use decrypted metadata.
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:47:I would still allow optional plugin-rendered preview later, but not as the default. If you allow it, constrain it brutally: fixed host-provided viewport, no dynamic height, no network during preview render unless explicitly allowed, no arbitrary chrome, no assumption that it appears for every row. The first usable version should not depend on WebView-per-row at all.
.triad\35-codex-ux.txt:73:- or the plugin requests `markRead()` during detail view after a host-approved event.

.triad\33-codex-review-log.txt:3819:.\docs\crypto-spec.md:88:> **V1 contract change (M5.1):** previous draft of this spec required `message_id`, `created_at`, and `schema_version` in AAD — those fields are server-generated and unknowable to the sender at signing time, so they have been removed from both AAD and the envelope. They remain server metadata stored next to the message but are not signed by the sender. AAD and the envelope are intentionally distinct: AAD authenticates context to AES-GCM (no ciphertext); the envelope is what Ed25519 signs (AAD + ciphertext + nonce). Replay protection is enforced at the server via the per-sender nonce registry (see §7).
.triad\33-codex-review-log.txt:3821:.\docs\crypto-spec.md:153:envelope_json = {"encrypted_body":"<base64 ciphertext_with_tag>","expires_at":"2026-05-20T00:00:00Z","min_plugin_version":"1.0.0","nonce":"<base64 nonce>","plugin_id":"plugin.weather","sender_id":"sender-alpha","user_id":"user-123"}
.triad\33-codex-review-log.txt:3822:.\docs\crypto-spec.md:156:The sender signs the canonical envelope JSON with Ed25519. The server reconstructs the envelope from the request fields and verifies against the registered sender's public key. Envelope is also what the recipient device uses to verify the signature on inbox fetch — therefore inbox/detail responses include `expires_at` (in addition to the body, nonce, signature).
.triad\33-codex-review-log.txt:3827:.\docs\crypto-spec.md:218:The V1 server performs a per-sender in-memory LRU replay check for the last 100,000 nonces. This protects the current server process only. A Redis or database-backed replay check is planned for V1.5.

docs\ROADMAP.md:12:- [x] Native Material 3 template renderer (no-JS plugins via JSONPath manifest)
docs\ROADMAP.md:14:- [x] Host-owned plugin settings sheet (sender mute, pairing revoke). Sender-side plugin-row revoke (`client.revoke_plugin(...)`) is a separate published-side API; the host UI exposes pairing revoke, not plugin-row revoke.
docs\ROADMAP.md:20:1. **Multi-process plugin host.** Move JS plugin execution to a dedicated Android process via AIDL/Binder. Isolates a crashing or runaway plugin from the host UI process and gives us a clean ProcessRecord boundary for the OS to kill independently.
docs\ROADMAP.md:21:2. **Native Kotlin plugin runtime (out-of-process).** A second plugin format alongside the JS bundle: a signed AAR loaded into the isolated process. Performance + platform-API parity for plugin authors who want a native experience, with the IPC boundary preventing in-process ClassLoader poisoning.
docs\ROADMAP.md:24:5. **Durable nonce-replay protection.** Move the per-sender nonce registry from in-memory to Postgres so a worker restart can't accept a replayed envelope.
docs\ROADMAP.md:28:DX track surfaced by external plugin-author review (lottery-claude, consultation 68). Phase 4.1 closed the small doc/validator wins; this section tracks the larger items that need design or app-side UX work.
docs\ROADMAP.md:30:6. **Automated pairing handshake.** Today the user reads `user_id` + `pairing_key_hex` off the device confirmation screen and types them into the sender's backend. V1.5 wires the bootstrap exchange end-to-end: the device POSTs the `(user_id, pairing_key)` tuple to a sender-supplied broker URL embedded in the QR; the sender's backend polls a `did the user complete this token?` endpoint and unlocks `set_pairing` automatically. Removes the largest first-impressions papercut. See `sdk-python/README.md` Quickstart for the V1 dogfood flow this replaces.
docs\ROADMAP.md:31:7. **Full round-trip example plugin.** `sdk-plugin/examples/minimal/` exists today but only exercises the plugin side; `examples/trading-bot/` exists but only exercises the sender side. V1.5 ships a `trading-bot` example with **both** a plugin and a backend in the same directory, sharing a `state.json`, ready to `./build.sh && python bot.py loop` and produce a real card on a paired device. Sets the floor for what plugin authors expect to copy.
docs\ROADMAP.md:32:8. **`npm create @syncler/plugin` scaffold.** Generator that produces a starter plugin (manifest, `src/plugin.ts` with sensible defaults, `build.sh`, `manifest.signed.json` placeholder, an example sender) so new authors aren't reading the integration guide line-by-line. Builds on #7.
docs\ROADMAP.md:33:9. **Validator polish backlog.** Centralize "things the SDK validator should reject that we currently let through to the server's 422." Examples: cross-renderer field conflicts already caught (`renderer: 'script'` + `template`), but not yet caught — `card_key` not matching `card_key_path`, illegal characters in plugin `id`, malformed semver components. Each addition reduces the server-side surface area and shortens the iteration loop for new authors.
docs\ROADMAP.md:38:11. **`message.respond` + `showNotification`.** Promote action callbacks from a fire-and-forget POST to a request/response handshake the plugin can await. Add a `showNotification` bridge so non-inbox-mode flows (background alerts) work.
docs\ROADMAP.md:40:13. **Script-fast runtime (Javy/QuickJS).** A lighter, non-WebView execution mode for purely logic-heavy plugins that don't need DOM. Faster cold-start and less memory than the current WebView path.
docs\ROADMAP.md:44:14. **WebSocket two-way channel.** Bidirectional, low-latency channel between plugins and their backends; covers chat, live cursors, presence. Complements but does not replace the SSE hint channel for V1's pull-then-render model.
docs\ROADMAP.md:45:15. **`platform.live.subscribe(...)` for script plugins.** API for a script bundle to subscribe to a named feed and receive incremental updates without polling.
docs\ROADMAP.md:47:17. **Redis pub/sub for SSE scaling.** Move the in-process event bus to a shared backplane so the SSE fanout works across multiple FastAPI workers and pods.
docs\ROADMAP.md:52:19. **Per-plugin user preferences.** Notification cadence, label overrides, delivery-window scheduling, do-not-disturb integration — all surfaced from the host's settings sheet without plugin-side code.

docs\integration-guide.md:3:You are integrating your app with the Syncler platform. Syncler is a content-blind transport + plugin host: your backend pushes end-to-end-encrypted blobs to a user's phone, and your plugin renders them. **Read this end-to-end before writing code; it's ~9 pages.**
docs\integration-guide.md:5:Syncler does not know what your data looks like, what your UI says, or what your buttons do. Your payload, your plugin code (or template manifest), your callback endpoint — Syncler just routes bytes.
docs\integration-guide.md:12:- **Template** (`renderer: "template"`) — no JavaScript. You ship a small JSON manifest that maps fields out of your payload via JSONPath (`$.title`, `$.body.summary`) into a native Material 3 card. Buttons are host-rendered and the host POSTs the decrypted payload to the action's declared endpoint. Faster, lighter, no JS attack surface; constrained to a fixed set of layouts.
docs\integration-guide.md:19:In every case you also need **backend code** that publishes the plugin once and sends payloads on demand, plus (for script renderer) an HTTP endpoint that receives the action callback.
docs\integration-guide.md:25:Once a card is in a user's inbox, the host handles all of the following without you doing anything plugin-side:
docs\integration-guide.md:32:- **Mute sync** — the user can mute your plugin globally (all devices) or locally (this device only) from the host-owned settings sheet. Muted plugins are hidden from the active inbox but remain accessible in the Senders tab for unmuting.
docs\integration-guide.md:34:- **Revocation UX** — if you revoke a plugin version with a `compromised` reason, the host refuses to execute the bundle on the device and shows a security banner instead. See §5.5.
docs\integration-guide.md:37:- **Real-time hints over SSE.** Foreground devices keep an authenticated Server-Sent Events stream open to `/v1/events`. The server pushes content-blind hints — `inbox.changed`, `state.changed`, `dismiss`, `card.upsert`, `card.delete` — and the client re-fetches over REST in response. The actual payloads still flow over the existing pull endpoints; SSE is the wakeup signal, not the data channel. Backgrounded devices fall back to FCM.
docs\integration-guide.md:43:### Payload (your backend -> user device)
docs\integration-guide.md:45:Two parts: a reserved **`hostPreview`** block the platform renders natively as the inbox row, and the rest of the payload that your plugin's `render()` consumes for the detail view.
docs\integration-guide.md:61:That JSON is your `payload` argument to `client.send_to(...)`. The plugin's `render(payload)` receives the entire dict — including the `hostPreview` block — but it doesn't need to use it (the host already drew the row).
docs\integration-guide.md:72:Total serialized `hostPreview` <= 2048 UTF-8 bytes. Missing block -> the row falls back to *"New message from {sender_name}"*. Senders **should** include it.
docs\integration-guide.md:74:`searchText` is folded into the host's inbox search alongside title, subtitle, summary, and sender name. The host does a case-insensitive substring match — populate this with the terms a user would type into the search box but that aren't part of the visible row (ticker symbols, ticket numbers, account references). Plugin-scoped *content* search (a search bar that runs against your backend) is V1.5.

docs\integration-guide.md:180:### 3.1 `declaredEndpoints` glob syntax
docs\integration-guide.md:182:`declaredEndpoints` controls which URLs your plugin may pass to `platform.network.fetch`. The host (both Android `EndpointMatcher.kt` and the TS SDK's `network.ts`) implements the same glob grammar:
docs\integration-guide.md:190:Example: to allow both `/api/v1/ack` and `/api/v2/ack`, declare both: `["https://example.com/api/v1/*", "https://example.com/api/v2/*"]`. Or, if you don't care about path depth, declare `["https://example.com/api/*", "https://example.com/api/*/*"]` and so on.
docs\integration-guide.md:192:This is the single most common manifest mistake — a plugin author declares `https://example.com/api/*` and then `fetch`-es `https://example.com/api/v1/users`, which gets rejected with `endpoint_not_declared`. The same pattern controls **template action endpoints** (the publish-time validator uses the same matcher to verify `action.endpoint` is in `endpoints`).
docs\integration-guide.md:200:  - `endpoint_not_declared` — URL doesn't match any `declaredEndpoints` glob (see §3.1).
docs\integration-guide.md:203:- **Timeouts**: two layers. The host's OkHttp client uses library defaults (~10s connect / 10s read each). The plugin bridge wraps every call in a hard 30s ceiling — even if the underlying HTTP request is still streaming, the bridge cancels and rejects the JS promise after 30 seconds. Plan UX around the 30s upper bound; if your endpoint is reliably slower, you need a redesign (e.g. async ack endpoint that the plugin polls).

docs\integration-guide.md:331:**Actions.** Each action is a button on the card. When tapped, the host POSTs the **full decrypted payload as `application/json`** to `endpoint`. No `Authorization` header is attached — the host explicitly uses an unauthenticated HTTP client for action POSTs so the user's Syncler bearer token never reaches your plugin endpoint (security boundary). If you need request authentication, HMAC the body using a sender-specific secret your backend knows out of band, or rely on TLS + endpoint allowlisting alone.

This lets the inbox merge multiple upserts for the same logical card into one row.

docs\integration-guide.md:444:`user_id` is **required**. The delete endpoint matches exactly that triple in the row lookup.

docs\integration-guide.md:495:## 7. The action callback endpoint
docs\integration-guide.md:508:**Template renderer** — the host POSTs the **full decrypted payload** as the body, verbatim. There's no JS hook to intervene. Your endpoint receives the whole payload JSON; pull whatever fields you need server-side. No `Authorization` header is added by the host (the user's Syncler bearer token never reaches your endpoint — security boundary). If you need request authentication, HMAC the payload using a sender-specific secret your backend shares out of band with itself, or rely on TLS + endpoint allowlisting alone.
docs\integration-guide.md:510:Note: the platform does **not** inject `device_id` into the body for you in V1. If you need to distinguish which device acted, include a sender-generated identifier in your payload and (for script renderer) have the plugin echo it back. For template renderer, your `card_key` (or any other field in the payload) serves the same dedupe role. Dedupe by `item_id` / `card_key` alone if a single tap is what you care about.
docs\integration-guide.md:513:- Status 409 -> idempotent dedupe (already saw this item); platform may retry on transient network errors so be ready.
docs\integration-guide.md:514:- Status 4xx other -> platform surfaces a generic failure to the plugin's promise.
docs\integration-guide.md:523:        return "", 409
docs\integration-guide.md:533:3. From your backend, run a script that registers your sender and prints a pairing QR (see SDK README).
docs\integration-guide.md:535:5. After confirming, the app shows your `user_id` and a `pairing_key_hex`. Copy both and feed them into your backend's `client.set_pairing(user_id, pairing_key=bytes.fromhex(...))` so it can encrypt for you.
docs\integration-guide.md:536:6. Publish your plugin (`client.publish_plugin(...)`) — save the `plugin_row_id`.
docs\integration-guide.md:538:8. The server pushes an `inbox.changed` event over the SSE stream the open app is subscribed to (foreground); the phone refreshes its inbox, decrypts the message, fetches your bundle (or applies your template manifest), and shows the native row within ~1s. If the app is backgrounded, FCM wakes it and the inbox refresh happens on the next foreground.
docs\integration-guide.md:539:9. Tap the row to open the plugin-rendered detail view, then tap your action button.
docs\integration-guide.md:547:- **`410 plugin missing, revoked, or not owned by sender`** — `plugin_id` (the row UUID) is wrong, or you accidentally used `plugin_identifier`. The two are distinct (see §5 vs §6).
docs\integration-guide.md:549:- **`409 nonce already used`** — your code reused a nonce somehow; SDK generates fresh nonces per `send_to`, so this means you're calling `send_to` with cached envelope bytes. Rebuild.
docs\integration-guide.md:550:- **`429 rate limited`** — back off + retry per `Retry-After` header.
docs\integration-guide.md:551:- **`410 plugin missing, revoked, not live-type, or not owned by sender`** (cards upsert) — the plugin row's `card_type` isn't `"live"`, or the row has a non-null `revoked_at`, or the publish row's `sender_id` doesn't match the signed envelope's sender. The error message is intentionally generic so a probing caller can't distinguish the cases (don't leak which plugin UUIDs exist).
docs\integration-guide.md:555:- **`409 sequence_number not greater than existing`** (cards upsert) — the existing card has a `sequence_number` ≥ what you sent. Sequence must be strictly increasing per `card_key`.
docs\integration-guide.md:557:- **`422 manifest validation failed`** (cards/plugins publish) — Pydantic rejected the manifest body. The detail field tells you which check failed. Most common: `template required when renderer == 'template'`, `card_key_path required when card_type == 'live'`, `action endpoint not allowed by declared endpoints`.
